description: Psychology grounding for the psychology/wellbeing lens — cognition, learning, motivation, and the well-replicated effects (avoiding the replication-crisis casualties).

entries.title: Classical & operant conditioning
entries.summary: Classical conditioning associates a neutral stimulus with a reflexive response (Pavlov's bell→salivation). Operant conditioning shapes voluntary behavior by consequences: reinforcement increases it, punishment decreases it, and variable-ratio reinforcement (slot machines) is the most persistent. These are the best-replicated laws of behavior and the basis of habit formation and reward design.

entries.title: Memory: encoding, storage, retrieval
entries.summary: Memory has working (limited, ~seconds) and long-term stores; information moves in via encoding and is recovered via retrieval cues. The forgetting curve is steep then flattens; spaced repetition and active retrieval (testing) beat re-reading. Memory is reconstructive, not a recording — it's editable and suggestible, which is why eyewitness testimony is fragile.

entries.title: Cognitive biases & dual-process thinking
entries.summary: Kahneman's System 1 (fast, intuitive) and System 2 (slow, deliberate) explain systematic errors: anchoring, availability, confirmation bias, loss aversion. Heuristics are usually efficient but misfire predictably. Knowing the bias rarely removes it — structural debiasing (checklists, base rates, outside view) works better than willpower.

entries.title: Motivation: intrinsic vs extrinsic
entries.summary: Self-determination theory holds that intrinsic motivation thrives on autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Extrinsic rewards can crowd out intrinsic interest (the overjustification effect) when they feel controlling. Durable engagement comes from meaningful challenge and progress, not just points — a direct lesson for game and habit design.

entries.title: Attachment & relationships
entries.summary: Early caregiver bonds form attachment styles (secure, anxious, avoidant) that shape adult relationships and stress regulation. Secure attachment predicts resilience; the styles are tendencies, not destiny, and shift with experience and reflective work. A well-replicated framework for social and emotional dynamics.

entries.title: Stress, the fight-or-flight response & allostatic load
entries.summary: Acute stress triggers the sympathetic fight-or-flight response (adrenaline, cortisol) that's adaptive short-term but corrosive when chronic — accumulating 'allostatic load' that harms sleep, immunity, and cognition. The Yerkes–Dodson curve: some arousal aids performance, too much degrades it. Recovery (sleep, parasympathetic activation) is not optional.

entries.title: Cognitive-behavioral model
entries.summary: The CBT model: thoughts, emotions, and behaviors interact, so distorted automatic thoughts (catastrophizing, all-or-nothing) drive distress, and changing thoughts or behaviors changes feeling. It's among the most empirically supported therapeutic frameworks; behavioral activation and cognitive reframing are its core, evidence-backed tools.

entries.title: Social influence & conformity
entries.summary: Behavior bends to the group: Asch showed conformity to obvious-wrong majorities; norms, authority, and social proof powerfully shape action. Diffusion of responsibility dampens bystander helping. These social forces explain crowd behavior, adoption curves, and why default options and visible norms change behavior more than information alone.

entries.title: Flow & the challenge-skill balance
entries.summary: Csikszentmihalyi's flow is the absorbed, time-distorting state arising when challenge matches skill, with clear goals and immediate feedback. Too easy → boredom; too hard → anxiety. Designing tasks (and games) that scale difficulty to growing skill keeps people in the flow channel — the engagement sweet spot.

entries.title: Cognitive development & the growth mindset
entries.summary: Cognition matures in stages (Piaget) and learning is scaffolded within a 'zone of proximal development' (Vygotsky) — just beyond current ability, with support. Believing ability is malleable (growth mindset) supports persistence, though effects are modest and context-dependent; the robust core is that deliberate, feedback-rich practice drives skill.
